title: Bartleby the Scrivener A Story of Wall Street
author: Herman Melville
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Bartleby the Scrivener A Story of Wall Street" book by Herman Melville?
Options:
A. Even if no higher motivation can be found, basic self-preservation should prompt all people, especially those prone to anger, to be kind and caring towards others. At the very least, in this particular case, I tried to suppress my aggravated feelings toward the scrivener by generously interpreting his actions. The poor man, I thought, means no harm; furthermore, he has endured difficult times and should be given some leeway. I also strived to distract myself right away, while also comforting my own despondency. I attempted to imagine that later in the morning, whenever might be convenient for him, something would occur to lift my spirits.  
B. Mere self-interest, then, if no better motive can be enlisted, should, especially with high-tempered men, prompt all beings to charity and philanthropy. At any rate, upon the occasion in question, I strove to drown my exasperated feelings towards the scrivener by benevolently construing his conduct. Poor fellow, poor fellow! thought I, he dont mean any thing; and besides, he has seen hard times, and ought to be indulged. I endeavored also immediately to occupy myself, and at the same time to comfort my despondency. I tried to fancy that in the course of the morning, at such time as might prove agreeable to him.
C. If higher motives fail, basic self-preservation should still prompt all people, but especially the quick-tempered, to show charity and care for others. In this case, despite my aggravated feelings, I tried to drown my frustration with the scrivener by putting the best spin on his actions. The poor man, I thought, intends no harm; he has suffered, so should be given some leeway. I also immediately busied myself and tried to lift my mood, imagining something agreeable might occur later.
D. Sheer self-interest, when no nobler incentive can be found, ought to motivate even hot-tempered individuals to act with charity and goodwill toward others. Here, though exasperated with the scrivener, I aimed to smother my irritation by construing his behavior generously. The poor fellow means no offense, I told myself; he has faced adversity, so should be indulged. I also tried at once to occupy and cheer myself, fancying something might happen later to please me.
Answer:

options.A: Even if no higher motivation can be found, basic self-preservation should prompt all people, especially those prone to anger, to be kind and caring towards others. At the very least, in this particular case, I tried to suppress my aggravated feelings toward the scrivener by generously interpreting his actions. The poor man, I thought, means no harm; furthermore, he has endured difficult times and should be given some leeway. I also strived to distract myself right away, while also comforting my own despondency. I attempted to imagine that later in the morning, whenever might be convenient for him, something would occur to lift my spirits.
options.B: Mere self-interest, then, if no better motive can be enlisted, should, especially with high-tempered men, prompt all beings to charity and philanthropy. At any rate, upon the occasion in question, I strove to drown my exasperated feelings towards the scrivener by benevolently construing his conduct. Poor fellow, poor fellow! thought I, he dont mean any thing; and besides, he has seen hard times, and ought to be indulged. I endeavored also immediately to occupy myself, and at the same time to comfort my despondency. I tried to fancy that in the course of the morning, at such time as might prove agreeable to him.
options.C: If higher motives fail, basic self-preservation should still prompt all people, but especially the quick-tempered, to show charity and care for others. In this case, despite my aggravated feelings, I tried to drown my frustration with the scrivener by putting the best spin on his actions. The poor man, I thought, intends no harm; he has suffered, so should be given some leeway. I also immediately busied myself and tried to lift my mood, imagining something agreeable might occur later.
options.D: Sheer self-interest, when no nobler incentive can be found, ought to motivate even hot-tempered individuals to act with charity and goodwill toward others. Here, though exasperated with the scrivener, I aimed to smother my irritation by construing his behavior generously. The poor fellow means no offense, I told myself; he has faced adversity, so should be indulged. I also tried at once to occupy and cheer myself, fancying something might happen later to please me.
